title: Tension Pneumothorax - Trauma
chief_complaint: Stab wound to left chest. Difficulty breathing. Getting worse.

physical_exam.Chest: Left chest: 2cm stab wound in 4th intercostal space midclavicular line. Absent breath sounds left side. Hyperresonance to percussion. Trachea deviated to RIGHT. Distended neck veins (JVD).
physical_exam.Cardiac: Tachycardia. Heart sounds normal (no muffled - not tamponade).
physical_exam.ATLS: A: patent. B: absent left breath sounds. C: Hypotensive, tachycardic, JVD. D: alert. E: isolated chest injury.

hidden_data.cxr: Large left pneumothorax with tension physiology: Left lung collapsed, mediastinal shift to RIGHT, depressed left hemidiaphragm, tracheal deviation to right. Right lung normal. No hemothorax.
hidden_data.eFAST: No pericardial fluid. No intra-abdominal free fluid.

correct_diagnosis: Tension Pneumothorax from Penetrating Chest Trauma - IMMEDIATE NEEDLE DECOMPRESSION
key_learning_points: Tension pneumothorax = one-way valve allowing air into pleural space but not out → progressive accumulation → ipsilateral lung collapse → mediastinal shift → contralateral lung compression → decreased venous return → obstructive shock → DEATH if untreated

CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS: Do NOT wait for CXR. Classic: Absent breath sounds + Hyperresonance + Tracheal deviation (AWAY from affected side) + JVD + Hypotension + Tachycardia. Respiratory distress and hypoxia

Immediate treatment: NEEDLE DECOMPRESSION: 14G angiocatheter, 2nd intercostal space midclavicular line OR 5th ICS anterior axillary line (ATLS 10th edition recommendation). Listen for rush of air (confirmatory). Leave catheter in place. IMMEDIATELY followed by tube thoracostomy (chest tube): 28-32Fr, 5th ICS anterior axillary line, directed posteriorly and superiorly. Connect to underwater seal (NOT suction initially)
If needle decompression fails (chest wall thick): Perform finger thoracostomy (2cm incision, blunt dissection into pleural space) - more reliable than needle in adults

Complications: Re-expansion pulmonary edema (uncommon but severe - from rapid lung re-expansion). Air leak (bronchopleural fistula if persistent). Infection (empyema if sterile technique not used). Tube malposition. Pulmonary laceration
Always check CXR after chest tube placement. Tube removed when: Lung fully expanded, no air leak for 24h, drainage <200mL/day. Remove during expiration or Valsalva
